title: Monetary Policy Report—January 2026
summary: The Bank of Canada held its target for the overnight rate at 2.25% (bank rate at 2.5%, deposit rate at 2.20%) as outlined in its January 2026 Monetary Policy Report. US tariffs continue to disrupt the Canadian economy, with growth expected to remain modest at around 1.25% over the projection horizon while inflation stays close to the 2% target. Risks to the outlook are described as 'unusually high' due to geopolitical uncertainty and the ongoing review of the Canada–United States–Mexico Agreement.
key_points: Bank of Canada maintained overnight rate target at 2.25%, with bank rate at 2.5% and deposit rate at 2.20%
CPI inflation remains near the 2% target, with excess supply offsetting upward cost pressures
Economic growth projected to average around 1.25% over the projection horizon
US tariffs remain elevated and continue to weigh on the Canadian economy alongside slowing population growth
Canadian businesses are expanding exports to countries other than the United States and reducing reliance on US imports
Global growth expected to remain solid, supported by AI investment boom and fiscal stimulus in major economies
Risks described as 'unusually high' due to geopolitical uncertainty and CUSMA review uncertainty
why_it_matters: This quarterly monetary policy report provides the official outlook of the Bank of Canada's Governing Council, including the rate decision, economic projections, and risk assessment. The held rate at 2.25% signals a cautious approach amid elevated uncertainty from US tariffs and trade agreement negotiations. Markets should monitor the balance between modest growth and controlled inflation against the risks of trade disruption.
macro_context: The Bank of Canada projects Canadian GDP growth of approximately 1.25% with inflation near 2% over the projection horizon. Excess supply in the economy roughly offsets upward cost pressures, maintaining inflation close to target. Global conditions are expected to remain supportive due to AI-driven investment and fiscal stimulus in major economies. Trade policy uncertainty—particularly around US tariffs and CUSMA—is the dominant risk factor.
